Shearwater 0.1.0
  • Reference
  • Articles
    • importing-and-preparing-gps-data
    • Movement Metrics
    • seabird_fisheries_overlap_workflow
    • Individual, Population, and Conservation Summaries
    • Trip Segmentation and Movement Behavior

Individual, Population, and Conservation Summaries

Source: vignettes/summaries.Rmd
summaries.Rmd
library(Shearwater)
library(dplyr)
library(geosphere)
library(sf)

Overview

This vignette picks up at the end of the movement-metrics pipeline. By this point your data have been read in, cleaned, and segmented into foraging trips (Vignettes 1–3). Here we:

  1. Aggregate per-trip metrics to individual and population level.
  2. Estimate each bird’s foraging range as a spatial polygon.
  3. Map the raw tracks.
  4. Export summary tables and GIS layers for reporting.

Standard column names used throughout the package:

Concept Column name
Individual ID track_id
Timestamp (UTC) datetime_gmt
Longitude longitude
Latitude latitude
At-colony flag at_colony
Trip ID trip_id
Behavioural phase phase

1 Load and standardise the example data 

gps_path <- system.file(
  "extdata",
  "example_wtsh_data.csv",
  package = "Shearwater",
  mustWork = TRUE
)

raw <- read.csv(
  gps_path,
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

tracks <- raw %>%
  mutate(
    track_id = "bird_001",
    datetime_gmt = as.POSIXct(
      paste(Date, Time),
      format = "%m/%d/%Y %H:%M:%S",
      tz = "UTC"
    ),
    longitude = Longitude,
    latitude = Latitude
  ) %>%
  arrange(track_id, datetime_gmt)

# Colony location — use first GPS point for this example
colony <- c(
  lon = tracks$longitude[1],
  lat = tracks$latitude[1]
)

tracks <- tracks %>%
  mutate(
    dist_to_colony_m = geosphere::distHaversine(
      cbind(longitude, latitude),
      matrix(
        c(colony["lon"], colony["lat"]),
        nrow = n(),
        ncol = 2,
        byrow = TRUE
      )
    ),
    at_colony = dist_to_colony_m <= 75
  )

head(tracks)
##        Date     Time Latitude Longitude Altitude Speed Course Type Distance
## 1 8/15/2013 20:01:59 21.57396 -158.2765    99.20     0      0   -2     0.00
## 2 8/15/2013 20:05:33 21.57431 -158.2762    -4.37  9144    146    0    46.38
## 3 8/15/2013 20:09:26 21.57412 -158.2764    38.35  1584    291    0    27.01
## 4 8/15/2013 20:12:32 21.57435 -158.2762    -6.62     0     60    0    34.85
## 5 8/15/2013 20:15:38 21.57437 -158.2760   -24.37     0    333    0    17.49
## 6 8/15/2013 20:18:43 21.57406 -158.2765   -50.77     0     80    0    57.53
##   Essential track_id        datetime_gmt longitude latitude dist_to_colony_m
## 1         1 bird_001 2013-08-15 20:01:59 -158.2765 21.57396          0.00000
## 2         1 bird_001 2013-08-15 20:05:33 -158.2762 21.57431         46.43322
## 3         1 bird_001 2013-08-15 20:09:26 -158.2764 21.57412         19.87951
## 4         1 bird_001 2013-08-15 20:12:32 -158.2762 21.57435         54.04253
## 5         1 bird_001 2013-08-15 20:15:38 -158.2760 21.57437         67.09004
## 6         1 bird_001 2013-08-15 20:18:43 -158.2765 21.57406         11.44986
##   at_colony
## 1      TRUE
## 2      TRUE
## 3      TRUE
## 4      TRUE
## 5      TRUE
## 6      TRUE

2 Build the per-trip metrics table

Cleaning, segmentation, and individual metric calculation are covered in Vignettes 2–3. summarize_movement() runs the full pipeline in one call and returns a named list containing every intermediate result.

results <- summarize_movement(
  track           = tracks,
  colony_coords   = colony,
  bird_id_col     = "track_id",
  datetime_col    = "datetime_gmt",
  lon_col         = "longitude",
  lat_col         = "latitude",
  colony_flag_col = "at_colony",
  duration_units  = "hours",
  include_spatial = TRUE
)

# Unpack the results list
trip_metrics       <- results$trip_metrics
individual_metrics <- results$individual_metrics
population_metrics <- results$population_metrics
foraging_ranges    <- results$foraging_ranges

For the rest of this vignette we use a representative mock table built from realistic Wedge-tailed Shearwater values so the code runs without the full upstream pipeline.

# Distances / path lengths in metres; duration in hours (set by duration_units).
trip_metrics <- tibble::tribble(
  ~track_id,  ~trip_id, ~trip_duration, ~trip_distance_m,
              ~path_length_m, ~max_distance_from_colony_m,
  "WTSH_001", 1L,  33.6,  312400,  485200,  68300,
  "WTSH_001", 2L,  38.8,  445200,  621000,  89400,
  "WTSH_002", 1L,  18.0,  189300,  285100,  42100,
  "WTSH_002", 2L,  36.0,  398700,  591200,  78900,
  "WTSH_002", 3L,  21.0,  215600,  318400,  51300,
  "WTSH_003", 1L,  18.0,  178400,  261500,  38700,
  "WTSH_004", 1L,  42.0,  421500,  628900,  85200,
  "WTSH_004", 2L,  38.0,  387200,  578100,  79600,
  "WTSH_005", 1L,  18.0,  195800,  291400,  45200
)

glimpse(trip_metrics)
## Rows: 9
## Columns: 6
## $ track_id                   <chr> "WTSH_001", "WTSH_001", "WTSH_002", "WTSH_0…
## $ trip_id                    <int> 1, 2, 1, 2, 3, 1, 1, 2, 1
## $ trip_duration              <dbl> 33.6, 38.8, 18.0, 36.0, 21.0, 18.0, 42.0, 3…
## $ trip_distance_m            <dbl> 312400, 445200, 189300, 398700, 215600, 178…
## $ path_length_m              <dbl> 485200, 621000, 285100, 591200, 318400, 261…
## $ max_distance_from_colony_m <dbl> 68300, 89400, 42100, 78900, 51300, 38700, 8…

3 Individual-level summaries — summarize_individual_metrics()

summarize_individual_metrics() collapses the per-trip table to one row per bird. For each metric column it computes the mean, maximum, and running total across that bird’s trips.

individual_metrics <- summarize_individual_metrics(
  trip_metrics     = trip_metrics,
  bird_id_col      = "track_id",
  trip_id_col      = "trip_id",
  distance_col     = "trip_distance_m",
  duration_col     = "trip_duration",
  path_length_col  = "path_length_m",
  max_distance_col = "max_distance_from_colony_m"
)

A readable summary of the key metrics (converted to km for display):

individual_metrics |>
  transmute(
    track_id,
    n_trips,
    mean_trip_dist_km    = round(mean_trip_distance_m / 1000, 1),
    max_trip_dist_km     = round(max_trip_distance_m  / 1000, 1),
    mean_trip_dur_hr     = round(mean_trip_duration, 1),
    max_trip_dur_hr      = round(max_trip_duration, 1),
    mean_max_colony_km   = round(mean_max_distance_from_colony_m / 1000, 1)
  )
## # A tibble: 5 × 7
##   track_id n_trips mean_trip_dist_km max_trip_dist_km mean_trip_dur_hr
##   <chr>      <int>             <dbl>            <dbl>            <dbl>
## 1 WTSH_001       2              379.             445.             36.2
## 2 WTSH_002       3              268.             399.             25  
## 3 WTSH_003       1              178.             178.             18  
## 4 WTSH_004       2              404.             422.             40  
## 5 WTSH_005       1              196.             196.             18  
## # ℹ 2 more variables: max_trip_dur_hr <dbl>, mean_max_colony_km <dbl>

What the columns mean

Column Description
n_trips Number of complete foraging trips recorded
mean_trip_distance_m Mean straight-line distance per trip
max_trip_distance_m Longest single trip
total_trip_distance_m Cumulative distance across all trips
mean_trip_duration Mean trip length (units set by duration_units)
max_trip_duration Longest single trip
total_trip_duration Total time away from colony
mean_path_length_m Mean cumulative track length per trip
mean_max_distance_from_colony_m How far out the bird typically goes
max_distance_from_colony_m Furthest point ever reached

Interpreting individual variation 

individual_metrics |>
  summarise(
    dist_range_km  = paste0(
      round(min(mean_trip_distance_m) / 1000, 0), " – ",
      round(max(mean_trip_distance_m) / 1000, 0), " km"),
    dur_range_hr   = paste0(
      round(min(mean_trip_duration), 1), " – ",
      round(max(mean_trip_duration), 1), " hr"),
    radius_range_km = paste0(
      round(min(mean_max_distance_from_colony_m) / 1000, 0), " – ",
      round(max(mean_max_distance_from_colony_m) / 1000, 0), " km")
  )
## # A tibble: 1 × 3
##   dist_range_km dur_range_hr radius_range_km
##   <chr>         <chr>        <chr>          
## 1 178 – 404 km  18 – 40 hr   39 – 82 km

Even in this five-bird sample, mean trip distance spans a nearly two-fold range. Birds making longer, farther trips may be accessing distant prey patches or may have fewer constraints on time away from the nest (e.g. failed breeders). Always check breeding-status metadata before drawing ecological conclusions from individual variation.

4 Population-level summaries — summarize_population_metrics()

summarize_population_metrics() takes individual_metrics and produces a single-row colony snapshot. It auto-detects every numeric column and computes mean, SD, min, and max for each, appending the statistic as a suffix.

population_metrics <- summarize_population_metrics(
  individual_metrics = individual_metrics,
  bird_id_col        = "track_id"
)

The raw output is wide (mean/SD/min/max for every individual-level column). Select the columns most relevant to your reporting context:

population_metrics |>
  select(
    n_individuals,
    starts_with("n_trips"),
    starts_with("mean_trip_distance_m"),
    starts_with("mean_trip_duration"),
    starts_with("mean_max_distance_from_colony_m")
  ) |>
  mutate(across(contains("_m_"), ~ round(.x / 1000, 1))) |>  # convert m → km
  glimpse()
## Rows: 1
## Columns: 17
## $ n_individuals                        <int> 5
## $ n_trips_mean                         <dbl> 1.8
## $ n_trips_sd                           <dbl> 0.83666
## $ n_trips_min                          <int> 1
## $ n_trips_max                          <int> 3
## $ mean_trip_distance_m_mean            <dbl> 285
## $ mean_trip_distance_m_sd              <dbl> 103.3
## $ mean_trip_distance_m_min             <dbl> 178.4
## $ mean_trip_distance_m_max             <dbl> 404.4
## $ mean_trip_duration_mean              <dbl> 27.44
## $ mean_trip_duration_sd                <dbl> 10.23074
## $ mean_trip_duration_min               <dbl> 18
## $ mean_trip_duration_max               <dbl> 40
## $ mean_max_distance_from_colony_m_mean <dbl> 60.5
## $ mean_max_distance_from_colony_m_sd   <dbl> 19.6
## $ mean_max_distance_from_colony_m_min  <dbl> 38.7
## $ mean_max_distance_from_colony_m_max  <dbl> 82.4

Reporting-ready text 

cat(sprintf(
  paste0(
    "Across %d individuals (%d total trips), Wedge-tailed Shearwaters at\n",
    "Kaena Point made foraging trips lasting %.1f \u00b1 %.1f hr (mean \u00b1 SD)\n",
    "and covering %.0f \u00b1 %.0f km. On average, birds reached a maximum\n",
    "of %.0f \u00b1 %.0f km from the colony."
  ),
  population_metrics$n_individuals,
  population_metrics$n_trips_mean * population_metrics$n_individuals,
  population_metrics$mean_trip_duration_mean,
  population_metrics$mean_trip_duration_sd,
  population_metrics$mean_trip_distance_m_mean / 1000,
  population_metrics$mean_trip_distance_m_sd   / 1000,
  population_metrics$mean_max_distance_from_colony_m_mean / 1000,
  population_metrics$mean_max_distance_from_colony_m_sd   / 1000
))
## Across 5 individuals (9 total trips), Wedge-tailed Shearwaters at
## Kaena Point made foraging trips lasting 27.4 ± 10.2 hr (mean ± SD)
## and covering 285 ± 103 km. On average, birds reached a maximum
## of 61 ± 20 km from the colony.

Sample size note. Population-level means are only as reliable as the individuals behind them. Aim for ≥ 10 tracked birds before presenting colony-level conclusions, and inspect individual_metrics for outliers first.

5 Estimating foraging range — calc_foraging_range()

calc_foraging_range() computes the spatial extent of each bird’s foraging activity, returning an sf polygon object. Two methods are available:

  • "convex_hull" — minimum polygon enclosing all foraging points (default).
  • "bbox" — axis-aligned bounding box; faster but always rectangular.

If phase_col is supplied, only points labelled with foraging_value are included. Without a phase column, all track points are used.

# Requires classify_trip_phase() to have been run first (adds the phase column)
foraging_ranges <- calc_foraging_range(
  track_data     = processed_track,
  bird_id_col    = "track_id",
  lon_col        = "longitude",
  lat_col        = "latitude",
  phase_col      = "phase",          # omit to use all fixes
  foraging_value = "foraging",
  method         = "convex_hull",
  crs            = 4326,
  area_crs       = 3857              # projected CRS used for area calculation
)
foraging_ranges |>
  arrange(desc(foraging_range_km2)) |>
  mutate(foraging_range_km2 = round(foraging_range_km2, 0))
## # A tibble: 5 × 3
##   track_id n_points foraging_range_km2
##   <chr>       <int>              <dbl>
## 1 WTSH_001     1472               4821
## 2 WTSH_004     1603               4413
## 3 WTSH_002     1504               2915
## 4 WTSH_005      372               2156
## 5 WTSH_003      361               1824

Joining foraging range to the individual summary

Because calc_foraging_range() returns one row per track_id, it joins cleanly onto individual_metrics:

individual_metrics |>
  left_join(
    foraging_ranges |> select(track_id, foraging_range_km2),
    by = "track_id"
  ) |>
  transmute(
    track_id,
    n_trips,
    mean_max_colony_km = round(mean_max_distance_from_colony_m / 1000, 1),
    foraging_range_km2 = round(foraging_range_km2, 0)
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(foraging_range_km2))
## # A tibble: 5 × 4
##   track_id n_trips mean_max_colony_km foraging_range_km2
##   <chr>      <int>              <dbl>              <dbl>
## 1 WTSH_001       2               78.8               4821
## 2 WTSH_004       2               82.4               4413
## 3 WTSH_002       3               57.4               2915
## 4 WTSH_005       1               45.2               2156
## 5 WTSH_003       1               38.7               1824

Birds with larger foraging ranges tend to have higher maximum colony distances, but the convex hull also expands when a bird visits many spatially separated areas within the same overall radius — making range area a complementary metric to maximum distance rather than a substitute for it.

6 Visualising tracks — plot_tracks()

A track map is the quickest sanity check after running the pipeline and a practical communication tool for non-technical audiences.

Column name note. plot_tracks() expects columns named lon and lat. Rename them from the package standard (longitude / latitude) before plotting.

{r plot-tracks, fig.width=6.5, fig.height=5.5, fig.alt="Map of WTSH GPS tracks coloured by individual, with colony marked."} tracks |> rename(lon = longitude, lat = latitude) |> plot_tracks( color_by = "track_id", colony_coords = colony, title = "Wedge-tailed Shearwater foraging tracks\nKaena Point, Oahu — August 2013" )

For a closer look at a specific trip, use plot_trip_map() (see the trip-segmentation vignette):

cleaned |>
  rename(lon = longitude, lat = latitude) |>
  plot_trip_map(
    trip_ids      = 1L,
    colony_coords = colony,
    title         = "WTSH_001 — Trip 1"
  )

7 Exporting results

7.1 Summary tables — export_policy_summary_tables()

export_policy_summary_tables() writes summary data frames to CSV for reports, databases, or collaborators who do not use R.

# Individual-level summary (one row per bird)
export_policy_summary_tables(
  data      = individual_metrics,
  file_path = "outputs/wtsh_individual_metrics.csv"
)

# Population-level snapshot (one row)
export_policy_summary_tables(
  data      = population_metrics,
  file_path = "outputs/wtsh_population_metrics.csv"
)

Both files are plain CSV and can be opened in Excel or imported into any statistical software. The population summary is compact enough to include directly in a species assessment; the individual summary supports the underlying statistical analyses.

7.2 Spatial layers — export_gis_layers()

export_gis_layers() writes sf objects to GIS-ready formats (GeoPackage by default). Use this to share foraging-range polygons with GIS analysts or to load them into QGIS or ArcGIS for overlay with management boundaries.

# Export foraging-range convex hulls
export_gis_layers(
  spatial_object = foraging_ranges,
  file_path      = "outputs/wtsh_foraging_ranges.gpkg"
)

# Export cleaned track points
export_gis_layers(
  spatial_object = sf::st_as_sf(
    cleaned,
    coords = c("longitude", "latitude"),
    crs    = 4326
  ),
  file_path = "outputs/wtsh_tracks.gpkg"
)

CRS check. export_gis_layers() calls assert_crs() internally. If your sf object has no CRS assigned, set one before exporting: sf::st_crs(foraging_ranges) <- 4326

Summary of functions used

Function Input Output
summarize_individual_metrics() One-row-per-trip metrics table One-row-per-bird: mean, max, total of each metric
summarize_population_metrics() Individual metrics table Single row: mean/SD/min/max of all individual metrics
calc_foraging_range() Track points (+ optional phase column) sf polygon per bird with foraging_range_km2
plot_tracks() Track data with lon/lat columns ggplot2 map
export_policy_summary_tables() Any summary data frame CSV file on disk
export_gis_layers() sf object GeoPackage / shapefile on disk

Next steps

  • Kernel utilization distributions: use estimate_kernel_ud(), get_core_area(), and get_home_range() for a probabilistic alternative to the convex-hull foraging range (Vignette 5).
  • Fisheries overlap: pass foraging_ranges and track data into analyze_fisheries_overlap() to quantify bycatch exposure by gear type (Vignette 6).
  • Jurisdiction overlay: run analyze_jurisdiction_overlap() to determine how much foraging time falls inside EEZs versus the high seas (Vignette 7).

Developed by Cooper Owen.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
